turns.text: Hello Andrea, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat, tucking hair behind ear*

Yeah, okay. Thank you for asking me to do this. Honestly, I'm... I'm still getting used to talking about it out loud, you know?

*pause*

I guess the biggest thing right now is that I want this so badly, but I'm also terrified. Like, I'm only twenty, and my friends are worried about midterms, and I'm over here thinking about folic acid and whether my body is going to be "good enough" for a baby. The doctor said I'm medium risk because of my family history with high blood pressure, and it just... it stuck with me in a weird way. Like my body already has a strike against it before anything even happens.

*looks down*

And then there's the practical stuff, which honestly might be even harder. Getting to that appointment today took forever — I had to transfer twice, and the train was late. I was sitting there sweating, thinking, "If I can't even make it to a doctor's appointment on time, how am I going to handle all the appointments when I'm actually pregnant?" Plus, I work part-time, and finding good food that doesn't cost a fortune in my neighborhood... it's not easy.

*voice gets quieter*

I lost my mom last year. She would've helped me figure all this out. So right now, it feels pretty lonely, if I'm being real with you. But I still want this. I do.
Imagine you've just decided with your partner — or on your own — that you'd like to start trying for a baby. Walk me through what that moment felt like and what your very first steps were.

*shifts uncomfortably, fidgets with hands*

Oh... um. Well, I should probably tell you — there's no partner right now. That's kind of the thing, you know? I'm not... I'm doing this alone. Or I will be, if I do it.

*takes a breath*

So it's not like I woke up with someone and we decided together or anything romantic like that. It's more like... I've been thinking about it for a while. Like, really *wanting* it. And then one day I just decided, you know? I was on the train — I spend a lot of time on the train — and I thought, "Why am I waiting? Why am I putting my life on hold?" I'm healthy enough, I have a job, and even though it's not perfect, I want to be a mom.

*looks up briefly*

So my first step was actually making that appointment at the clinic. I was nervous as hell calling them. Like, what if they judged me for being alone? But the nurse was actually really nice. That's when she told me about the folic acid and all the risk stuff.

*voice wavers slightly*

Sometimes I wonder if I'm being selfish or crazy, you know? Like, is it fair to a baby to do this without someone there? But then I think about my Mami and how hard she worked for me alone, and I think... maybe I can do this too.

*pause*

I just wish I didn't have to figure it all out by myself.
You mentioned taking those first steps — what made you choose that particular action rather than something else, like speaking to a doctor first or doing your own research online?

*rubs face tiredly*

Honestly? I did both, kind of at the same time. Like, I was already googling stuff at like midnight — you know how that goes. You start reading one thing and then three hours later you're down some rabbit hole about vitamins and complications and... yeah.

*laughs nervously*

But the thing is, when you're reading stuff online, it's all so... generic? And scary. Everyone's situation is different, but the internet treats it like there's one right way to do everything. I needed to talk to an actual person who knew about *me* — my family history, my situation. That's why I called the clinic.

*leans forward slightly*

I think I was also looking for permission, honestly. Like, if a doctor said "okay, this is safe, you should do this," then maybe I wouldn't feel so crazy. Maybe it would feel more real, more legitimate. Because in my head, there's this voice telling me I'm too young, too alone, not ready. And I needed someone in a white coat to tell me I wasn't completely messing up by thinking about this.

*voice gets quieter*

Plus, my Mami used to always say you talk to the doctors, you trust the professionals. That stuck with me, even though she's not here to actually help me do it.

*meets your eyes*

The research helped, but... I needed to hear from someone real.

Imagine you've booked a preconception appointment with your GP and you're sitting in the waiting room. What's going through your mind as you wait, and what are you hoping will happen in that consultation?

*clasps hands together, legs bouncing slightly*

Oh man, okay. So I'm sitting there, right? And the waiting room is beige and cold, and there's this woman across from me with a toddler who keeps throwing a toy, and I'm just... watching her. Like, she looks tired but also so *capable*, you know? And I'm thinking, "Can I be like that? Will I know what to do?"

*voice gets smaller*

I'm nervous the doctor is going to tell me I'm not ready. Or that there's something wrong with me already. I keep checking my phone — I'm paranoid I'm going to miss my name being called because the clinic is loud and I'm not paying attention. Getting here was stressful enough.

*takes a breath*

What I'm hoping for... I guess I want someone to tell me this is possible. That being twenty and alone doesn't automatically make me a bad mother. I want them to say, "Okay, here's what we need to do to make sure you and your baby are healthy," and have it feel like a *plan* instead of just... chaos.

*looks down at hands*

I also hope they don't make me feel stupid for not knowing things. And I'm hoping they'll actually listen when I tell them about the transportation thing, the food access thing. Not just hand me a pamphlet and send me out.

*voice wavers*

Mostly though, I just want to feel like someone believes in me. That this isn't impossible.
When you imagined how that appointment would go, how did you picture the GP responding to you — and how did reality compare, if you've had that experience?

*nods slowly*

Yeah, I actually went. That's what I was telling you about earlier — today, actually.

*settles back in seat*

So going in, I was imagining... I don't know, maybe someone older, kind of maternal? Someone who would sit down and really *talk* to me, not just check boxes. I was worried they'd judge me or act like my age was the problem, or that being alone was irresponsible. Like they'd ask me about a partner and then... I don't know, give me that look.

*pauses*

The nurse was actually really kind about that part. She didn't judge. She asked about my family history, my health, all that stuff in a way that felt... respectful, I guess? She actually listened when I mentioned the transportation issues and the grocery situation. She didn't just brush past it.

*but voice becomes frustrated*

But then it was also kind of... clinical? She gave me all these terms and statistics — "medium risk," "neural tube defects" — and I could see she cared, but it felt like information *at* me instead of... with me, if that makes sense? Like, she told me to eat fresh foods and take folic acid, which is great, but she didn't really help me figure out *how* when I can barely get across the city.

*looks up*

And I didn't get that feeling of someone being in my corner, you know? It felt more like... checking a box on a form.

Imagine you've been trying to conceive for several months without success. A close friend suggests you try a fertility app she found helpful. How do you react to that suggestion, and what do you do next?

*shifts uncomfortably, looks away*

Okay, so... I haven't actually been trying for months yet. I'm still in the preconception phase, like, preparing to try. But I can imagine how that would feel, yeah.

*fidgets with sleeve*

Honestly? I think I'd be a little embarrassed at first. Like, my friend is saying this so casually, but in my head it becomes this big thing — like, "Oh, so I'm not getting pregnant, there's something *wrong*." You know? It makes it feel more real and more scary.

*pauses*

But also... I'd probably download it. Because what else am I gonna do? And the app thing feels safer somehow than asking a doctor, especially after today's appointment where everything felt so heavy and clinical. An app is just... on my phone. Private. I can figure things out at my own pace without someone telling me my body is "medium risk."

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

Though if I'm being honest, I'd probably become obsessed with it. Like, tracking everything, looking at the data constantly, getting my hopes up every cycle. And then when nothing happens... I don't know. I think I'd spiral a little.

*meets your eyes*

The thing is, I don't really have close friends here to talk to about this stuff. So if someone did suggest something, I'd probably grab onto it like a lifeline, even if I wasn't totally sure about it.

Imagine you've just had a difficult conversation with your partner about whether you're both truly ready to have a child — financially, emotionally, practically. What does 'being ready' actually mean to you, and who or what helped you form that picture?

*pauses, looks down*

I... I need to be real with you about this. There's no partner. There's no conversation like that happening because I'm doing this alone.

*fidgets with hands*

I know that might sound weird given the scenario, but I can't really pretend about that part. It's the whole thing, you know? Like, me having a partner would solve so much — someone to talk to at night, someone to help with money, someone to be in the delivery room. But that's not my reality right now.

*voice gets quieter but more honest*

But to answer your question about what "being ready" means... I think for me it's different than it would be for someone with a partner. I'm not waiting for us to have the conversation about finances or whether we both want this. I'm just... figuring out if *I* can do it alone.

*looks up*

Being ready means having enough money to not panic every time there's a doctor's bill. It means having someone — like a friend, a family member, *someone* — who checks in and helps. It means not feeling like I'm failing before I even start.

*voice becomes more reflective*

My Mami... she showed me what doing it alone looks like. She worked so hard, and she made it work. So my picture of "ready" comes from her, I guess. It's not about having it all perfect. It's about having enough strength and enough support to get through.

*pause*

I'm still not sure I have enough of either.
When you think about the financial side of readiness, were there specific worries — like maternity leave, childcare costs, housing — that felt most pressing? Where did those concerns come from?

*leans forward, elbows on knees*

Oh God, yeah. The money stuff keeps me up at night, honestly.

*counts on fingers*

So right now I'm working part-time, which means I don't have maternity leave. Like, if I take time off to have a baby, I just... don't get paid. And then there's childcare — I looked into it once and nearly had a heart attack. In Boston, it's like thousands of dollars a month. How am I supposed to afford that on part-time wages?

*voice tightens*

And housing. I'm renting a tiny studio that's already tight on my budget. If I need to move to somewhere bigger for a baby, that's more money. Everything is more money.

*rubs face*

A lot of this comes from watching my Mami struggle, honestly. She'd work these long shifts and then come home stressed about whether she could pay rent and buy groceries. I remember her worrying out loud, and even though she tried to hide it, I *knew*. And I swore I wouldn't put a kid through that.

*voice wavers*

But here I am, about to do the same thing, basically. The clinic nurse kept talking about prenatal vitamins and fresh foods, and I'm thinking, "Do you know how much that costs?" Nobody asked me if I could actually afford to do everything they're recommending.

*looks at you directly*

Sometimes I wonder if wanting this baby is selfish because I can't give it the stability it deserves.

Imagine you've experienced a previous pregnancy loss and are now considering trying again. You're sitting with that knowledge as you think about the future. What thoughts and feelings come up, and what would you need from a healthcare team to feel supported going forward?

*goes very still, eyes get glassy*

Oh... okay.

*takes a moment, voice becomes quiet and careful*

I lost my Mami last year. That's... that's the loss I carry. Not a pregnancy loss, but it's still a loss, you know? And sometimes I wonder if that's part of why I'm thinking about having a baby so much. Like, I'm trying to fill this hole that she left.

*clears throat*

But if I'm being honest, the idea of being pregnant and then losing that baby... I can't even think about it without feeling sick. Because I'd be doing it alone. I wouldn't have my mom there saying, "It's not your fault, mija. Your body didn't fail." I'd just be... alone with that grief on top of everything else.

*voice strengthens slightly*

So if I ever did have a loss — God, I hope I don't — what I'd need from doctors is... gentleness. And real time. Not just a fifteen-minute appointment where they tell me my body is "medium risk" anyway. I'd need someone to sit with me and say, "This is hard. You're grieving. We're going to help you through this."

*looks directly at you*

And I'd need them to understand that for me, having a baby is about more than just biology. It's about healing from losing my mom. It's about creating family when mine feels broken.

*voice cracks slightly*

I'd need them to believe in me, even when I don't believe in myself.
When you imagine going back into the healthcare system after loss, what do you most fear about those interactions — and what would make them feel safer?

*wraps arms around self*

God, that's... that's a hard question.

*takes shaky breath*

I think what I fear most is being treated like a statistic. Like, "Oh, you're twenty, unmarried, medium risk — of course something went wrong." I'm already carrying that "medium risk" label around like it's stamped on my forehead. If I lost a baby, I'd be terrified a doctor would look at me like I *knew* this would happen, like I was foolish for trying in the first place.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

And I'd be scared they wouldn't take my grief seriously because... I don't know, because I'm young? Because I don't have a partner to validate my pain? Like, my grief only counts if someone else is grieving with me?

*pauses, collecting thoughts*

What would make it safer... I think having the same person. Not rotating through different doctors who don't know my story. Someone who knows I lost my mom, who knows I'm alone, who gets that this baby meant everything to me. Someone who checks in after, not just medically but like... actually asks how I'm doing emotionally.

*looks up*

And I need them to listen when I say I want to try again. Don't tell me to wait. Don't make me feel like I'm being reckless. Just... help me. Work *with* me, not at me.

*voice quieter*

And maybe... maybe help me find a support group or something. People who understand what this feels like.

Imagine you have a pre-existing health condition and you're wondering how it might affect a future pregnancy. You decide to look it up before speaking to anyone medical. What do you search for, and how does what you find make you feel?

*nods, pulls out phone almost reflexively*

Yeah, okay. So I already kind of did this because of the hypertension thing in my family. I was googling late at night, like 2 AM, which is never a good idea.

*scrolls through imaginary phone*

I'd search something like "high blood pressure pregnancy risks" or "can I have a healthy baby with family history of hypertension." And then the rabbit hole starts, right? You click one link and suddenly you're reading about preeclampsia and gestational hypertension and all these complications that sound terrifying.

*voice becomes anxious*

Some websites made it sound like having family history means it's basically guaranteed to happen to me. Other sites were like "it's fine, just monitor it," which isn't reassuring because now I'm paranoid about monitoring it. And there's always someone's story in the comments — "I had high blood pressure and lost my baby" — and that just... it stays with you.

*looks up, frustrated*

The worst part is that none of it felt personalized. It's all this general information that could apply to anyone, but I'm sitting there thinking "but what about *me specifically*?" And Dr. Google can't answer that.

*voice wavers*

It made me feel more scared, honestly. More like my body is working against me before I even start. Which is probably why I need to talk to a real person about it, but after that appointment today... I'm not sure if I trust that anyone's actually going to help me feel less afraid.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*sits back, takes a moment to think*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*voice becomes more urgent*

I feel like today we talked a lot about the scary stuff — the risks, the money, being alone. And all of that is real. But I want you to know that underneath all of that fear, there's something else. There's this... this hope, I guess? Like, I *want* this so badly. I want to be a mom. I want to hold my baby and teach them things and be there for them in ways my Mami couldn't always be there for me because she was working so hard.

*looks directly at you*

And I think healthcare people need to understand that too. That we're not just coming in as problems to solve or risks to manage. We're coming in as people who are dreaming about something really important.

*voice becomes more steady*

The other thing is... nobody's asked me what *I* need. Like, the clinic gave me pamphlets and told me what to do, but nobody sat down and asked "Andrea, what do you actually need to feel supported through this?" Because the answer isn't just folic acid. It's knowing someone cares enough to ask. It's having a plan that actually works for my life, not some generic plan.

*pause*

And maybe... maybe if healthcare felt less lonely and scary, more people like me would actually follow through. We wouldn't give up.

*quieter*

That's what I'd want you to know.
